task_name: Hand Motion Analysis for Laparoscopic Surgery Skill Assessment
content_summary: SAGES 2015 concurrent session presentation by Munenori Uemura, PhD on a novel skill evaluation system based on hand motions during laparoscopic surgery performance. The presentation covers background on hand motion analysis (HMA), methods using chaos neural networks and machine learning, and results from blind testing on expert vs. novice surgeons.
reason_not_scored: This is an educational/research presentation with narration, slide overlays, data visualizations, and discussion of methodology. It is NOT a video recording of an actual trainee or expert performing an FLS task (peg transfer, pattern cut, endoloop, extracorporeal suture, or intracorporeal suture). Therefore, no FLS performance scoring is applicable.

teaching_content.research_topic: New Skill Evaluation System Based on Hand Motions During Laparoscopic Surgery
teaching_content.primary_focus: Using hand motion analysis and machine learning (chaos neural networks) to distinguish expert from novice laparoscopic surgeons
demonstrated_techniques: Hand motion analysis (HMA) capturing paths of center of gravity (COG)
Detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA) for motion stability assessment
Unstable periodic orbits (UPO) analysis for hand motion characterization
Chaos neural network system for skill classification
research_methods: Input factors: COG paths, relative paths, velocity of COG, relative velocity, orbital characteristics of hand motion
Test cohort: 12 expert surgeons (>500 laparoscopic cases) vs. 17 novice surgeons (<15 cases)
Output: Expert vs. Novice classification via optimized neural network
Success rate: 79.3% correct judgment rate in distinguishing expert from novice
key_findings: Neural network trained on hand motion factors could accurately distinguish expert from novice surgeons
System achieved 79.3% correct classification rate on blind test data
All 12 expert surgeons correctly identified; 16 of 17 novices correctly identified
Future work mentioned: improving judgment rate with more data, integrating into skill assessment tools
future_applications: Real-time surgical skill assessment during training
Skill Rank system for objective suturing skill evaluation
Integration of motion analysis with visual assessment metrics (stability of COG, flexibility of maneuver, bimanual coordination)
teaching_content.verbal_cues: Research presentation by PhD researcher; no live surgical demonstration
teaching_content.instructional_value: High for understanding objective assessment of laparoscopic surgical skill beyond traditional timing-based metrics; introduces biomechanical analysis as a novel evaluation approach

confidence_rationale: Classification is definitive: this is a peer-reviewed research presentation at SAGES 2015 with clear slide structure, methodology diagrams, results charts, and discussion. No FLS task performance is visible. The content is entirely educational/research-focused.
